subject: mathematics

core_competencies.justification: The chapter's entire organising arc is the discovery and geometric proof of the Baudhāyana-Pythagoras theorem using areas of squares erected on the sides of a right-angled triangle, with explicit citation of Baudhāyana's Śulba-Sūtra verses (1.9, 1.12, 1.13, 2.1) and three hands-on construction activities (A-1, A-2, A-3) that enact these geometric constructions directly.

Sections 2.1 and 2.2 develop strategies for computing areas of composite shapes formed by triangles and squares (doubling/halving constructions); section 2.7 applies area-of-square and area-of-triangle formulae to solve the Bhāskarāchārya Līlāvatī problem (E-15 type) and find missing sides in composite geometric figures including a rhombus (E-16) and equilateral triangle (E-22).

adjunct_competencies.justification: Section 2.4 requires reasoning about angle sums and congruence of right triangles to prove the combined four-sided figure is a square; exercises E-17 (is hypotenuse the longest side?), E-16 (rhombus sidelength from diagonals using perpendicularity), and E-15 (missing sides of triangles from given dimensions) all apply properties of triangles and quadrilaterals.

Section 2.3 establishes that √2 is a real number that cannot be expressed as a terminating decimal or as a fraction m/n with m, n positive integers, introducing the concept of irrational numbers as a distinct class within the real number system beyond the rationals.

The chapter is structured around the historical contributions of Baudhāyana (Śulba-Sūtra, c. 800 BCE) and Bhāskarāchārya (Līlāvatī); section 2.6 recounts Fermat's 17th-century conjecture and Andrew Wiles's 1994 proof, situating the theorem in a living mathematical tradition.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to prove and apply the Baudhāyana-Pythagoras theorem by discovering its geometric basis through area relationships between squares constructed on the sides of right-angled triangles.
